title: Capsella biformis
taxonomy: Capsella biformis is classified within the family Brassicaceae, commonly known as the mustard or cabbage family. This classification is based on the plant's botanical characteristics, such as its flower structure and leaf morphology. As a member of the Brassicaceae family, Capsella biformis is closely related to other plants like Arabidopsis thaliana and Arabis alpina. The family Brassicaceae is further classified within the order Brassicales, which is a group of flowering plants that share similar characteristics. Overall, the classification of Capsella biformis reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Brassicaceae family.
uses: Capsella biformis, also known as shepherds' cress or white pepperwort, has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant was used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The plant is rich in antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. However, more research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential benefits. Further studies are required to confirm its efficacy in treating specific health conditions.
constituents: Capsella biformis is not commonly known by that name. However, the plant Capsella bursa-pastoris, which is sometimes referred to by a similar name,  contains various active constituents including alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins. These compounds may contribute to its medicinal properties. Research on the plant is ongoing, and more studies are needed to determine the full extent of its active constituents. Further investigation may uncover additional compounds with potential health benefits.
side_effects: Capsella biformis, also known as shepherd's needle or twinpod, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. However, as with many plants, there are potential side effects associated with its use. Capsella biformis may cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, or interact with certain medications in individuals who are sensitive to its compounds. Furthermore, ingesting large quantities of the plant may lead to gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea or diarrhea. More research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of using Capsella biformis as a medicinal herb.
